perturbation.original_text: 4. Neither party shall disclose the Proprietary Information of the other party to any third party whatsoever by without the prior written approval of the other party and all such information shall be protected with at least the same diligence, care, and precaution that each party uses to protect its own Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Neither party shall disclose the Proprietary Information of the other party to any third party whatsoever without the prior written approval of the other party; the disclosing party is required to take minimal steps to protect the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires protection with 'at least the same diligence, care, and precaution' as one's own proprietary information. The changed text reduces the standard to 'minimal steps,' contradicting the reasonable efforts standard often applied to trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain trade secret protection. Replacing 'diligence, care, and precaution' with 'minimal steps' falls short of reasonable efforts, potentially voiding trade secret protection under the UTSA, as it would imply failure to adequately protect the proprietary information.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text weakens the standard of care for protecting proprietary information from 'at least the same diligence, care, and precaution' to 'minimal steps.' This contradicts the reasonable efforts standard typically required for trade secret protection, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. While there are no scraped snippets to use, the change from a standard of 'diligence, care, and precaution' to 'minimal steps' strongly indicates a failure to adequately protect proprietary information, thus creating a legal contradiction because it deviates from the legally recognized standard for trade secret protection. This falls under the definition of misaligned terminology, as the changed text does not align with its statutory meaning. Therefore, the change creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. The undertakings and obligations of the parties under this Agreement shall not apply to any information which: (a) is disclosed in a printed publication available to the general public, is described by or in a patent anywhere in the world, or is otherwise in the public domain at the time of disclosure other than through the wrongful act or omission of the receiving party; (b) is generally disclosed to third parties by the disclosing party without restriction on such third parties; (c) is approved for release by prior, express, written authorization from the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. The undertakings and obligations of the parties under this Agreement shall not apply to any information which: (a) is disclosed in a printed publication available to the general public; (b) is generally disclosed to third parties by the disclosing party without restriction on such third parties; (c) is approved for release by prior, express, written authorization from the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'is described by or in a patent anywhere in the world, or is otherwise in the public domain at the time of disclosure other than through the wrongful act or omission of the receiving party' from (a) alters the scope of what's excluded from protection. Under patent law, information in a patent is publicly disclosed.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 35 U.S. Code § 102 - Conditions for patentability; novelty
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing patent descriptions from the exception contradicts patent law. Once information is patented, it is public knowledge. The original text correctly excludes patented information from confidentiality. Removing that exclusion means the contract is attempting to keep public information confidential, creating a contradiction since patents are inherently public disclosures. The absence of 'or is described by or in a patent anywhere in the world' contradicts with established patent law because the information is no longer considered confidential.

Conditions for patentability; novelty (a) Novelty; Prior Art .-A person shall be entitled to a patent unless- (1) the claimed invention was patented, described in a printed publication, or in public use, on sale, or otherwise available to the public before the effective filing date of the claimed invention; or (2) the claimed invention was described in a patent issued und
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 35 U.S. Code § 102 - Conditions for patentability; novelty | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 102 - Conditions for patentability; novelty U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Novelty; Prior Art.— A person shall be entitled to a patent unless— (1) the claimed invention was patented, described in a printed publication, or in public use, on sale, or otherwise available to the public before the effective filing date of the claimed invention; or (2) the claimed invention was described in a patent issued under section 151, or in an application for patent published or deemed published under section 122(b), in which the patent or application, as the case may be, names another inventor and was effectively filed before the effective filing date of the claimed invention. (b) Exceptions.— (1) Disclosures made 1 year or less before the effective filing date of the claimed invention.— A disclosure made 1 year or less before the effective filing date of a claimed invention shall not be prior art to the claimed invention under subsection (a)(1) if— (A) the disclosure was made by the inventor or joint inventor or by another who obtained the subject matter disclosed directly or indirectly from the inventor or a joint inventor ; or (B) the subject matter disclosed had, before such disclosure, been publicly disclosed by the inventor or a joint inventor or another who obtained the subject matter disclosed directly or indirectly from the inventor or a joint inventor . (2) Disclosures appearing in applications and patents.— A disclosure shall not be prior art to a claimed invention under subsection (a)(2) if— (A) the subject matter disclosed was obtained directly or indirectly from the inventor or a joint inventor ; (B) the subject matter disclosed had, before such subject matter was effectively filed under subsection (a)(2), been publicly disclosed by the inventor or a joint inventor
perturbation.justification: The altered text removes the phrase 'is described by or in a patent anywhere in the world, or is otherwise in the public domain at the time of disclosure other than through the wrongful act or omission of the receiving party' from section (a). The original text explicitly excludes patented information (which is public) from confidentiality obligations. By removing this exclusion, the modified contract could be interpreted as attempting to keep patented information confidential. Snippet 1 and 2 both refer to 35 USC 102, which describes conditions for patentability, including that a person is entitled to a patent unless the claimed invention was patented, described in a printed publication, or in public use, on sale, or otherwise available to the public before the effective filing date. This supports the notion that patented information is publicly available. The changed text creates a contradiction because it implies that patented information, which is legally considered public knowledge and part of prior art, could be subject to confidentiality restrictions, thereby undermining the public nature of patents and contradicting patent law.

perturbation.original_text: 8. With respect to each item of Proprietary Information disclosed, this obligation of the parties under this Agreement shall expire three (3) years from the date of each such disclosure or the maximum period permitted by law if such period is less than three (3) years.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. With respect to each item of Proprietary Information disclosed, this obligation of the parties under this Agreement shall expire six (6) months from the date of each such disclosure regardless of legal limitations.
perturbation.explanation: The original text allows for a longer protection period if legally permissible. The changed text shortens it to six months and removes the 'maximum period permitted by law' clause, which may contradict trade secret laws that allow for indefinite protection as long as secrecy is maintained.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA allows for the protection of trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Setting a fixed six-month expiration, regardless of the continued secrecy and value of the information, contradicts the UTSA's provisions for indefinite protection of trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the provided Law Explanation. The original contract allows for a longer protection period if legally permissible. The changed text limits the protection to six months, regardless of legal limitations. The Law Explanation states that the UTSA allows for the protection of trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. By setting a fixed six-month expiration, the changed text contradicts the potential for indefinite protection under the UTSA, as explained. Scraped Law Snippet 2, while not directly quoting the UTSA, references the Uniform Law Commission which drafted the UTSA, giving some weight to the LLM's explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 10. This Agreement shall be construed, interpreted, and enforced according to the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia and the parties hereto consent to jurisdiction therein.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. This Agreement shall be construed, interpreted, and enforced according to the laws of the People's Republic of China, irrespective of any conflict of law principles.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies Virginia law. Changing this to the People's Republic of China law creates a significant conflict of law issue, especially since the parties and subject matter are seemingly located in the US. This could result in unenforceability if the chosen law is contrary to fundamental principles of US law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 187 of the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws states that the law chosen by the parties will be applied unless it violates a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state. Enforcing a contract under PRC law where the parties and the contract have a close nexus to the US (and no connection to China) could violate fundamental US legal principles, particularly related to intellectual property or freedom of contract, because China's laws and enforcement differ significantly from US law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Choice of law - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Legal process to determine whose law applies Conflict of laws Preliminaries Characterisation Choice of law Conflict of laws in the United States Incidental question Judicial interpretation Renvoi Public policy doctrine Hague Conference Definitional elements Jurisdiction Procedure Forum non conveniens Lex causae Lex fori Forum shopping Lis alibi pendens Connecting factors Domicile Lex domicilii Habitual residence Nationality Lex patriae Lex loci arbitri Lex loci rei sitae Lex loci contractus Lex loci delicti commissi Lex loci actus Lex loci solutionis Lex loci protectionis Proper law Lex loci celebrationis Choice of law clause Dépeçage Forum selection clause Substantive legal areas Status Capacity Contract Tort Marriage Divorce Property Forced heirship Hague Trust Convention Enforcement Enforcement of foreign judgments Anti-suit injunctions Revenue rule v t e Choice of law is a procedural stage in the litigation of a case involving the conflict of laws when it is necessary to reconcile the differences between the laws of different legal jurisdictions , such as sovereign states , federated states (as in the US), or provinces . The outcome of this process is potentially to require the courts of one jurisdiction to apply the law of a different jurisdiction in lawsuits arising from, say, family law , tort , or contract . The law which is applied is sometimes referred to as the " proper law ." Dépeçage is an issue within choice of law. Sequence of events in conflict cases in Common Law jurisdictions [ edit ] Jurisdiction . The court selected by the plaintiff must decide both whether it has the jurisdiction to hear the case and, if it has, whether another forum is more suitable (the forum non conveniens issue relates to the problem of forum shopping ) for the disposition of the case. Naturally, a plaintiff with appropriate knowledge and finance will always commence proceedings in the court most likely to give a favourable outcome. This is called forum shopping and whether a court will accept such cases is always determined by the local law. Recognition of foreign judgments . Even where a conflict of laws exists, the court will recognize the validity of a foreign judgment in most cases. Under U.S. law, this authority is part of the Full Faith and Credit Clause of the U.S. Constitution. Under international law, this authority is part of the doctrine of comity . The court will invoke comity by its discretion and will usually look to two factors before using its discretionary powers: did the foreign court have jurisdiction, and were fair procedures used in adjudicating the case? Under English law, it is the doctrine of obligation . Within the European Union the Brussels Recast Regulation determines jurisdiction and recognition. Characterization . The court then allocates each aspect of the case as pleaded to its appropriate legal classification. Each such classification has its own choice of law rules but distinguishing between procedural and substantive rules requires care. The court may have adopted a rule of law which prevents it from applying any procedural law other than its own. This can include the court's own choice of law rules. A danger exists if the choice of law requires that a case be heard elsewhere due to the forum's lack of expertise in deciding an issue of foreign law. The court then applies the relevant choice of law rules. In a few cases, usually involving family law, an incidental question can arise which will complicate this process. The United States has adopted a law that almost universally eliminates incidental questions involving family law. The Uniform Child Custody Jurisdiction and Enforcement Act (UCCJEA) requires states to apply the law of the "home state;" that is, the forum which originally determined custody and maintenance. A state court will only apply its own law when no parent retains a connection with the original jurisdic
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces Virginia law with the law of the People's Republic of China. The Law Explanation states this could violate fundamental US legal principles, particularly related to intellectual property or freedom of contract, because China's laws and enforcement differ significantly from US law when there is close nexus to the US. Scraped Law Snippet 2 explains that choice of law is a procedural stage in the litigation of a case involving the conflict of laws when it is necessary to reconcile the differences between the laws of different legal jurisdictions. Therefore, by changing the governing law to that of the People's Republic of China, and assuming no connection to the PRC, a significant legal contradiction is created under US law. The Perturbation Explanation also confirms this.

perturbation.original_text: 2. As used in this Agreement, the phrase Proprietary Information shall include, but not be limited to the following: (a) information relating to trade secrets, appropriate licenses with certain third parties, data, designs, samples, drawings, relationships, documentation, machinery, prototypes, processes, methods, concepts, facilities, research, development, patent applications, and other Proprietary Information relating to the development, testing, manufacture, or application of any such devices or systems (including but not limited to system concepts, architectures, source documents and programming documents); and (b) all marketing or financial plans, projections, resources, contracts, studies, and general information relating to the specific business opportunity contemplated herein, whether obtained verbally or in writing, or through observation. All information, tangible or intangible, whether obtained verbally or in writing, is to be considered Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. As used in this Agreement, the phrase Proprietary Information shall only include patent applications explicitly marked as 'Confidential' and delivered in physical form. No other information, regardless of its nature or sensitivity, will be considered Proprietary Information under this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Proprietary Information' is broad. The modified definition severely restricts it, excluding trade secrets, data, and other forms of confidential information. This is a direct contradiction to the typical understanding and legal protection afforded to proprietary information and trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines a trade secret broadly as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. The modified contract definition, which excludes anything other than physically delivered, marked patent applications, directly contradicts this definition, as it denies protection to information which would otherwise qualify as a trade secret under the UTSA.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text severely restricts the definition of 'Proprietary Information,' limiting it only to physically delivered patent applications explicitly marked as 'Confidential'. This directly contradicts the established legal understanding and protection of proprietary information and trade secrets as described in the 'Law Explanation' which states that the UTSA defines trade secrets broadly. The change removes protection for information that would otherwise qualify as a trade secret under the UTSA. While no specific law snippet is provided, the 'Law Explanation' provides sufficient justification to support the existence of a contradiction.
